analogy: Think of "How When and Where" through the chapter's own opening images: Introduction: How, When and Where 1 How Important are Dates? There was a time when historians were fascinated with dates. There were heated debates about the dates on which rulers were crowned or battles were fought. In the common-sense notion, history was synonymous with dates. The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 2 OUR PASTS – III, INTRODUCTION: HOW, WHEN AND WHERE 3, 4 OUR PASTS – III, INTRODUCTION: HOW, WHEN AND WHERE 5. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
How When and Where is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that introduction: How, When and Where 1 How Important are Dates?

The text explains that there was a time when historians were fascinated with dates.

The text explains that there were heated debates about the dates on which rulers were crowned or battles were fought.

The text explains that in the common-sense notion, history was synonymous with dates.

The text explains that you may have heard people say, “I find history boring because it is all about memorising dates.” Is such a conception true?

The text explains that history is certainly about changes that occur over time.

The text explains that it is about finding out how things were in the past and how things have changed.

The text explains that as soon as we compare the past with the present, we refer to time, we talk of “before” and “after”.

The text explains that living in the world we do not always ask historical questions about what we see around us.

The text explains that we take things for granted, as if what we see has always been in the world we inhabit.

The text explains that but most of us have our moments of wonder, when we are curious, and we ask questions that actually are historical.

The text explains that watching someone sip a cup of tea at a roadside tea stall, you may wonder – when did people begin to drink tea or coffee?

The text explains that looking out of the window of a train you may ask yourself – when were railways built and how did people travel long distances before the age of railways?

The text explains that reading the newspaper in the morning you may be curious to know how people got to hear about things before newspapers began to be printed.

The text explains that 1 – Brahmans offering the Shastras to Britannia, frontispiece to the first map produced by James Rennel, 1782 Rennel was asked by Robert Clive to produce maps of Hindustan.

The text explains that an enthusiastic supporter of British conquest of India, Rennel saw preparation of maps as essential to the process of domination.

The text explains that 2 OUR PASTS – III All such historical questions refer us back to notions of time.

The text explains that sometimes it is actually incorrect to fix precise dates to processes that happen over a period of time.

The text explains that there can be no one clear date for a process such as this.

The text explains that similarly, we cannot fix one single date on which British rule was established, or the national movement started, or changes took place within the economy and society.

The text explains that why, then, do we continue to associate history with a string of dates?

The text explains that there was a time when history was an account of battles and big events.

The text explains that as you have seen in the history textbooks of the past two years, historians now write about a host of other issues, and other questions.

The text explains that they look at how people earned their livelihood, what they produced and ate, how cities developed and markets came up, how kingdoms were formed and new ideas spread, and how cultures and society changed.

The text explains that in separate chapters, we read about the deeds of others — Hastings, Wellesley, Bentinck, Dalhousie, Canning, Fig.

The text explains that all the dates in these history books were linked to these personalities – to their activities, policies and achievements.

The text explains that the chronology of their lives marked the different chapters of the history of British India.

The text explains that can we not write about the history of this period in a different way?

The text explains that how do we focus on the activities of different groups and classes in Indian society within the format of this history of Governor-Generals?

The text explains that when we write history, or a story, we divide it into chapters.

The text explains that in the process we focus only on those events that help us to give shape to the story we are telling.

The text explains that clearly, we need another format for our history.

The text explains that in 1817, James Mill, a Scottish economist and political philosopher, published a massive three-volume work, A History of British India.

The text explains that in this, he divided Indian history into three periods – Hindu, Muslim and British.

The text explains that can you think of any problem with this way of looking at Indian history?

The text explains that why do we try and divide history into different periods?

The text explains that we do so in an attempt to capture the characteristics of a time, its central features as they appear to us.

The text explains that according to his telling of history, before the British came to India, Hindu and Muslim despots ruled the country.

The text explains that 3 – Warren Hastings became the first Governor-General in 1773 While history books narrated the deeds of Governor-Generals, biographies glorified them as persons, and paintings projected them as powerful figures.

The text explains that in this idea of history, British rule represented all the forces of progress and civilisation.

The text explains that in any case, can we refer to any period of history as “Hindu” or “Muslim”?

The text explains that moving away from British classification, historians have usually divided Indian history into ‘ancient’, ‘medieval’ and ‘modern’.

The text explains that it is a periodisation that is borrowed from the West where the modern period was associated with the growth of all the forces of modernity – science, reason, democracy, liberty and equality.

The text explains that medieval was a term used to describe a society where these features of modern society did not exist.

The text explains that you will see how they established control over the economy and society, collected revenue to meet all their expenses, bought the goods they wanted at low prices, produced crops they needed for export, and you will understand the changes that came about as a consequence.

The text explains that when the subjugation of one country by another leads to these kinds of political, economic, social and cultural changes, we refer to the process as colonisation.

The text explains that (b) Official documents help us understand what the people of the country think.

The text explains that (c) The British thought surveys were important for effective administration.

The text explains that what is the problem with the periodisation of Indian history that James Mill offers?

The text explains that why did the British preserve official documents?

The text explains that how will the information historians get from old newspapers be different from that found in police reports?

The text explains that can you think of examples of surveys in your world today?

Source: NCERT Class 8 Our Pasts III, Chapter 1: How When and Where

prelims_traps: Do not treat 2 OUR PASTS – III and INTRODUCTION: HOW, WHEN AND WHERE 3 as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to How When and Where; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

Use 2 OUR PASTS – III and INTRODUCTION: HOW, WHEN AND WHERE 3 to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
